revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ujisha]][[ja:氏社]]
'''Ujisha''' (氏社) refers to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} that enshrine a {{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|qid=Q767655|lt=ujigami|1=Ujigami}} (clan deity). == Overview == In ancient times, it was common for the leaders of communities to conduct rituals by enshrining local deities. These rituals sometimes took on a national character when performed by leaders recognized as {{ill|Uejō|ja|氏上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uejō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氏長者|1=Clan head|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11547822}} under the authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}. However, during this period, the deities worshipped by the clan leaders were not always the ancestral founders of their clans. Instead, the worship often focused more on deities related to the land or specific roles rather than bloodline connections. This was because ancient clans could be formed through both paternal and maternal lines, and individuals could belong to multiple kinship groups. Consequently, territorial ties and other factors influenced the religious affiliations and the determination of which ritual circles individuals belonged to. Entering the {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|qid=Q8086|lt=8th century|1=8th century}}, when the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} became firmly established, changes also occurred in the clan system. The {{ill|Gōzoku|en|Gōzoku|qid=Q84066|lt=aristocrats|1=Gōzoku}} of the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|qid=Q906708|lt=Kinai region|1=Kinai}} began to receive official Ranks in the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q124313007}}, becoming increasingly disconnected from their ancestral lands. Additionally, the establishment of the {{ill|Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in Asia|en|Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in Asia|qid=Q11621855|lt=Protected appointments|1=Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in Asia}} system further promoted the formation of paternal lineage groups. This led to the development of ujigami as guardian deities for those sharing the same paternal ancestor, along with the shrines (ujisha) that enshrined these deities. Representative examples include: - {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|qid=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}}, where the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|qid=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan|1=Fujiwara clan}} enshrined their guardian deities {{ill|Kashima deity|ja|1=Takemikazuchi|lt=Kashima-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima deity|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Katori deity|ja|香取神|1=Futsunushi|lt=Katori-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori deity|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 経津主神}}, as well as their ancestral deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|Ame-no-Koyane|qid=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and {{ill|Himegami|en|Himegami|qid=Q22070227|lt=Himegami|1=Himegami}}. - {{ill|Umenomiya Taisha|en|Umenomiya Taisha|qid=Q276781|lt=Umenomiya Taisha|1=Umenomiya Taisha}}, where the {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|qid=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan|1=Tachibana clan (kuge)}} enshrined the deity worshipped by their ancestress {{ill|Tachibana no Michiyo|ja|橘三千代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana no Michiyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 県犬養三千代|1=Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11582685}} as their clan’s guardian. - {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|en|Itsukushima Shrine|qid=Q191763|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|1=Itsukushima Shrine}}, which flourished alongside the rise of the {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|qid=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}} and became their clan deity. - {{ill|Hirano Shrine|ja|平野大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirano Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平野神社}}, enshrined by the maternal relatives and descendants of {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|en|Emperor Kanmu|qid=Q314846|lt=Emperor Kanmu|1=Emperor Kanmu}}. For the {{ill|Kawachi Genji|en|Kawachi Genji|qid=Q5360652|lt=Kawachi Genji|1=Kawachi Genji}}, their clan deity was enshrined at {{ill|Tsuboi Hachimangū|en|Tsuboi Hachimangū|qid=Q11429677|lt=Tsuboi Hachimangū|1=Tsuboi Hachimangū}}, while {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|qid=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}} in Kyoto was also revered. In 1180, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|qid=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} transferred the deity to its current location at {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|qid=Q701403|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}} in the northern hills of Kobayashi-go. These clans were all established or expanded under the Ritsuryō system, actively developing paternal lineage groups. Among the middle and lesser aristocratic clans of the Kinai region, as well as regional chieftains, traditional ujigami worship persisted. However, during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the establishment of the {{ill|manor and public land system|ja|荘園公領制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/manor and public land system|1=shōen-kokugaryō system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11618906}} led to changes in property and inheritance practices. As estates and wealth began to pass directly between fathers and sons, the traditional forms of clan deity worship weakened. Gradually, estate management shifted to be organized around individual households, with local territorial deities or {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|qid=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}} (deities of one's birthplace) being revered as ujigami. As this trend progressed, new ujisha were established throughout Japan. Furthermore, in many regions, the ujigami of various households were unified into a shared guardian deity for the entire community, known as a {{ill|chinju|ja|鎮守|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chinju|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鎮守神|1=Chinjugami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q97479976}}. This development eventually evolved into the contemporary relationship between local ujigami and {{ill|Ujiko|en|Ujiko|qid=Q125562175|lt=ujiko|1=Ujiko}} (parishioners). == References == * Osumi Kiyoharu, "Ujisha" in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 1 (Heibonsha, 1992), ISBN 978-4-582-13101-7.
{{translated page|ja|氏社|version=93431321|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:氏社]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Ujisha]]
